summary: McKenzie describes his experiment selling Bingo Card Creator software on eBay as a secondary sales channel. He dropped the price to $14.95 to compete with physical bingo card products, noting that eBay fees eat into margins but production and shipping costs remain zero. He acknowledges eBay sales won't scale meaningfully but views it as a way to test selling copy and generate small amounts of cash for advertising. The post reflects the scrappy, experimental mindset of a bootstrapper willing to try unconventional channels.
key_points: eBay fees (listing + percentage cuts) significantly reduce per-sale profit compared to direct website sales ($9-10 vs $20)
Zero marginal cost for digital products means even low-margin channels can be worth experimenting with
eBay serves as a testing ground for sales copy even though the audience differs from his target market
Early-stage bootstrappers need to generate any revenue they can to fund advertising and infrastructure
money_quotes: shipping cost is still zero and production costs are still zero, which means I still clear ~9-10 profit per sale
At current volumes, there is no way it will ever be worth my time.
it lets me quickly try out selling copy
Sure, I could fund this project with my day job until the sun goes nova, but whats the fun in that?
key_insight: Even unprofitable sales channels can serve as cheap testing grounds for copy and messaging when you're bootstrapping a software business.
